interactions.summary: Jiang reads Virgil's Trojan Horse as an anti-Greek allegory: Greek logic, philosophy, and theater are the real Trojan Horse that manipulates Troy, destroys culture, and exposes Homer's love and forgiveness as trickery in the Roman propaganda frame.
Aeneas wants to kill Helen and die with Troy, but Venus and the sign of his son's fiery crown redirect him from rage and suicide into duty toward his family and Rome's future.

Jiang contrasts a 'good wife' who erases herself for Aeneas' future with Dido, whose love traps Aeneas in Carthage. The gods command him back to destiny, Dido kills herself, and love becomes a disease that causes historical war.

Aeneas reaches Italy, is pulled into war with Turnus, and finally sees the belt taken from his friend. Jiang argues that the abrupt ending is finished because Aeneas' final spear thrust is the beginning of his transformation into duty rather than mercy.

speaker_notes.note: The transcript says Iliad where context indicates the Aeneid; Jiang is describing Virgil's Trojan Horse and Priam scenes.
The transcript renders Turnus as Churnus; the intended Aeneid character is Turnus.
Jiang uses deliberately provocative gendered language while reconstructing Virgil's propaganda logic; public prose should distinguish his source-grounded claim from endorsement.

claims.claim: In Jiang's reading, the real Trojan Horse in the Aeneid is Greek culture itself: logic, philosophy, and theater enter by beauty and persuasion and destroy the host culture from within.

Virgil's anti-Homeric message, as Jiang states it, is that if Romans embrace Greek logic, philosophy, and theater, Roman culture will be destroyed and must therefore resist Greek culture at all costs.
The Priam scene in the Aeneid reverses Jiang's Homeric reading: love, friendship, and forgiveness are presented as lies or tricks, while brutality and force triumph.

Aeneas' rage at Helen and desire to die with Troy are interrupted by divine and familial signs that redirect him to duty: leave Helen, return to family, and preserve the son who will found a great empire.
Jiang reads Creusa's disappearance or death as Virgil's model of the 'good wife': she removes herself so Aeneas can embrace Rome's future and avoid dishonor.
Dido represents love as a political catastrophe: her love for Aeneas makes him forget destiny, her suicide commands Carthage to destroy Rome, and the lecture links this to Hannibal's later war.
Jiang states the Roman message directly: love is a disease or plague on the world because Helen's love causes Troy and Dido's love causes Carthage's war with Rome.
claims.temporal_scope: Core Aeneid model in the 2024-11-21 lecture.

claims.claim: The Aeneid's final duel tests mercy against duty: Aeneas considers forgiving Turnus, sees the belt taken from his dead friend, and kills him.
Jiang rejects the view that the Aeneid's abrupt ending proves it unfinished; he argues the ending works because it completes Aeneas' character transformation.

signature_moments.moment: The real Trojan Horse is Greek culture.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The real Trojan Horse is Greek culture
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the lecture its cleanest image of cultural conquest as infiltration.

signature_moments.moment: Virgil's world has no place for love, friendship, or forgiveness; only brutality and force survive.
signature_moments.source_phrase: There is no place for love, friendship, and forgiveness
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the hard inversion of Jiang's Homeric thesis.

signature_moments.moment: The propaganda logic names the good wife as someone who will kill herself for her husband.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a good wife is someone who will kill herself for her husband
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows how duty erases love and individual desire in the Roman model.

signature_moments.moment: Love becomes a disease, a plague upon the world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Love is a disease, a plague upon the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This phrase carries the lecture's central Homer-to-Virgil reversal.

signature_moments.moment: Mercy stops at the belt; Aeneas plunges the spear into Turnus.
signature_moments.source_phrase: he just plunges his spear into Churnus
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The final image shows duty defeating the Homeric possibility of forgiveness.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The Virgilian propaganda image in which logic, philosophy, and theater enter beautifully but destroy Roman culture from within.
Jiang's name for the Aeneid's anti-Homeric model: love causes war, madness, and historical catastrophe.
The Roman imperative that redirects Aeneas from personal rage, death, or mercy toward Rome's future.
The Carthaginian queen whose love for Aeneas becomes Jiang's example of love as political disease and historical cause.
Aeneas' Italian rival; the final duel with him shows mercy being overruled by duty and revenge.

uncertainty_notes.note: The focus segment ends mid-phrase after 'it took the'; packet 0006 carries the rest of the character-transformation argument.
The transcript says Helen's staying home means 'this war would have started'; context indicates 'would not have started'.
